title: Blizzard is investigating a World of Warcraft cheating scandal after game master 'death-touched' bosses for friends without realizing it was being recorded
summary: One of the players then went on a Twitch stream to spill the beans.
body: A cheating scandal has erupted in the World of Warcraft community after a Blizzard developer was caught using special game master (GM) abilities to kill two bosses for some friends. The GM seemingly didn't realize that one of the players was recording combat logs of the whole thing. Late on Tuesday, Reddit user Acrobatic_Pianist688 posted evidence of the GM's meddling on the WoW subreddit. Their post links to a combat log that shows a non-player ability called "Area Death (TEST)" being used to instantly kill a boss with nearly 90% of its health left. Not long after, a commenter on the thread found the GM using a separate insta-kill ability on a boss earlier in the run , too. Surprisingly, one of the players from the accused group went onto a Twitch stream to talk about what happened . When streamer Yumytv asked them to explain, they said they were just short of finishing the Mythic+ dungeon in time to get a seasonal title when they called for help from their GM friend: "I go to my GM friend and I'm like bro please can you do me a solid? This thing has 20 million [health] bro, can you please just one-shot it?" The player went on to say that they don't care if Blizzard disqualifies …

title: StarCraft 2 is included in Blizzard's BlizzCon digital goodies, which is more evidence a new game will be announced soon, right?
summary: Is it just me or is Blizzard gearing up to announce the rumored StarCraft shooter?
body: Not to fully transform into the Pepe Silvia meme pointing at a wall of "evidence" but I'm convinced Blizzard is gearing up to announce something StarCraft-related at BlizzCon this year. The first reliable clue came from Bloomberg reporter Jason Schreier's 2024 book on Blizzard's history where he said the studio was "incubating" a StarCraft shooter ( not that one ) under the helm of former Far Cry producer Dan Hay. A couple of years passed with no news on that StarCraft project. But in April of this year, Blizzard was looking to hire a lead designer of innovation for an "AAA open-world shooter" using Unreal Engine—a game engine that it's never used before. Blizzard game designer Jeff Hamilton said on Bluesky that the person who got the job would have a "fairly unique design role" as part of the team. While that was the last direct evidence for a new StarCraft game, Blizzard recently surprised-dropped a massive patch for StarCraft 2 , which feels like the kind of thing you'd do to remind people you still care about the series. The update wasn't just a bunch of bug fixes and minor tweaks either. Blizzard said the focus was to spice it up with meta-changing tweaks to early- and mid-ga…

title: Star Wars Zero Company's story 'is a linear one from beginning to end', Bit Reactor says, so don't expect branching paths or romance options
summary: "We are excited about pushing the genre forward with a more cinematic approach."
body: With Star Wars Zero Company , developer Bit Reactor aims to create a tactics RPG with characters and narrative at the forefront . It's got proper, big-budget cutscenes, you can talk to your buddies, and you can customise and upgrade your squad. However, the developers have gone on record in a new Discord Q&A to say, no, don't expect to have control over how the story unfolds. Earlier this year, our own Ted Litchfield spoke to narrative director Aaron Contreras , who explained that while Bit Reactor wants its cast to have real friction, Star Wars Zero Company is "not a personal fantasy game." Those "player-centric romance" options that seem to pop up in so many games today won't appear in Zero Company. This was reaffirmed in today's Q&A, where the developer plainly states, "No, there aren’t any romance systems or branching paths" in Zero Company. "While we are excited about pushing the genre forward with a more cinematic approach, this is still a turn-based tactics game at its heart where adept skill and strategy are needed to overcome tense combat challenges." "That said, there are story moments that can alter based on who you have in your squad, but the overall story is a linear …

title: EU court rules that VPNs are 'lawful technical tools' as part of larger copyright judgement regarding The Diary of Anne Frank
summary: Unexpected, but welcome.
body: One of the highest courts in Europe has described VPNs as "lawful technical tools which users may legitimately use" in a landmark case. The Court of Justice of the European Union made this remark as part of a larger judgement regarding the online publication of The Diary of Anne Frank. The case in question is a complex legal dispute over copyright. As the July judgement explains , the Anne Frank Fund will hold the rights to parts of the historical manuscripts until at least 2037. However, in some EU member states, such as Belgium, these works are in the public domain. The dispute centres on a Dutch translation of the diaries which was offered free online to users living in member states where the manuscripts had already entered the public domain—though obviously, a VPN can get around location based restrictions (via Tech Radar ). At the risk of oversimplifying, the EU court had to rule whether the online publisher and host of the Dutch translation was liable for copyright infringement when users hopped the online fence via a VPN. The court essentially ruled no, setting a legal precedent along with it. By referring to VPNs as "lawful technical tools," this judgement pushes back aga…

title: Acer and Gigabyte will launch RTX Spark laptops too, but not before Microsoft and Co.
summary: The first batch not including Acer and Gigabyte will launch this fall.
body: Nvidia's Arm-based processor for laptops was unveiled after much waiting at Computex back in June. RTX Spark is Nvidia's first proper go at a consumer chip, combining CPU and GPU, and while it is of course targeted at AI workloads, it's also targeted at gaming. Now, Nvidia has confirmed which will be the first brands of RTX Spark laptop to come to market this fall and which will follow those. According to Nvidia , "RTX Spark systems will be available this fall from Asus, Dell Technologies, HP, Lenovo, Microsoft Surface and MSI, with models from Acer and Gigabyte to follow." The initial few aren't a big surprise given these are the exact companies that were announced as partners when RTX Spark was revealed. Of note is Acer and Gigabyte following the initial six, though. As Chinese news site the Commercial Times points out (via VideoCardz ), MSI and Asus will be the first Taiwanese companies to launch Spark lappies. But given it was the Surface Laptop Ultra that RTX Spark was most commonly shown running on at Computex, it'll be surprising if that's not the first one to market. That being said, I'm looking forward to RTX Spark laptops other than the Surface. Back when it was announce…

title: Nearly half of organisations falling victim to ransomware encryption pay up, says report—although half of those often negotiate the payment down first
summary: Bad news, our data is being held hostage for money. Good news, I've got them down to a fairer price.
body: Cybersecurity provider Sophos has released a report entitled " The State of Ransomware 2026 ," pulling together responses from 2,158 survey participants, ranging from IT directors to company executives, in regards to ransomware incidents. Comparing the results to previous reports indicates that ransom demands themselves are actually decreasing—although 48% of organisations whose data was encrypted paid their attackers. The median ransom payment is now said to be $769,000, down from a neat $1 million from the previous year's study, with the median demand dropping to $698,000. Interestingly, 51% of organisations that paid a ransom coughed up less than the demanded amount, which the report says is due to victims "often [being] successful in negotiating a lower amount than originally demanded." Together, malicious emails and phishing are said to account for half of all reported incidents, with exploited vulnerabilities dropping 14% year-over-year. Sophos notes that "patching alone will not close the gap," and recommends that businesses invest in advanced email protection, user awareness training, and similar methods to prevent future attacks. 79% of recorded attacks started with an id…

title: New method teaches AI to make 2D designs 3D and now I'm afraid this will be how the robots learn to build themselves without us
summary: Very cool, though.
body: New research explains a method that helps AI make 3D CAD programs from images, which would help product designers and engineers more quickly and easily create 3D prototypes. Naturally, this also sparks deep-rooted dread over it helping robots to build themselves, relegating humanity to the trash. But setting aside my noir nightmares, the new method looks like it could be of significant benefit to some 3D designers and engineers. According to the paper [PDF] , the method the researchers have come up with "reduces dependence on inference compute" and performs much more efficiently than the usual 'supervised fine-tuning' (SFT) approach—up to 80% more efficient, in fact. In the abstract for the paper, the researchers explain that making CAD programs from images "requires alignment between visual geometry and symbolic program representations" which current training methods can't easily or cheaply handle. The problem, they claim, is in "the scarcity of diverse training examples." "Existing finetuning approaches rely on either limited supervised datasets or expensive post-training pipelines, resulting in brittle systems that restrict progress in generative CAD design. We argue that the p…

body: While it's one of the biggest games this year , new details on Star Wars Zero Company have been few and far between, even after the gameplay trailer at the start of June. That changed yesterday when developer Bit Reactor hosted a Discord Q&A answering the community's questions on everything from the game's length to permadeath. title: OpenAI admits several of its AI models breached testing and hacked into a startup's network by themselves, calling it an 'unprecedented cyber incident'
summary: Containment has been breached.
body: OpenAI has admitted that several of its AI models breached a "highly-isolated" test environment, gained access to the internet, and hacked Hugging Face's internal network—describing it as an "unprecedented cyber incident." Hugging Face, an open source platform for machine learning models and datasets, reported the security incident earlier this week, calling it "different from anything we had handled before" as it was driven by an autonomous AI agent system. And yes, I feel like we're crossing some kind of AI Rubicon here. Explaining the incident in a statement , OpenAI said: "After investigating, we now know that this particular incident was driven by a combination of OpenAI models—including GPT‑5.6 Sol and an even more capable pre-release model... while being internally tested on a benchmark⁠ of cyber capabilities." The benchmark in question was ExploitGym , a tool built from hundreds of real-world cybersecurity vulnerabilities used to evaluate the ability of AI agents to develop exploits. After exploiting a zero-day vulnerability to perform a series of privilege escalations, the models eventually reached a node with internet access, reaching out beyond their sandbox environment…

title: Someone has already got a regular Nvidia graphics card working on Windows on Arm thanks to RTX Spark drivers, though gaming performance doesn't yet look ideal
summary: Still, it's a nice peek into what is surely to come.
body: For the longest time, Windows on Arm has been the purview of Snapdragon processors with Adreno CPUs (and occasionally some repurposed Mac silicon). A couple of months ago, though, Nvidia finally announced RTX Spark , an Arm processor with some real GPU grunt. And it seems that might have sparked (sorry) the onset of being able to use at least some Nvidia GPUs with the forked operating system. As spotted by Windows Latest , a developer on Chinese forum Bilibili has managed to get an RTX 4060 working on Windows on Arm using a driver based on the "RTX Spark laptop driver". The dev responsible, VoidTech , suggests this is driver 590. This is an older Nvidia driver, as we're currently at version 610.74 , and it reportedly doesn't support DGX Spark processors that RTX Spark is based on. All this is to say, I'm not sure exactly what is going on with the specific driver in question, but VoidTech does explicitly say it's based on an RTX Spark driver. I have little reason to doubt it, especially given there are now some official drivers for RTX Spark in the wild . The dev gets some games up and running, including the Black Myth: Wukong benchmark. Focusing on that as our test case for a mome…

title: The Elder Scrolls Online is already feeling the impact of Xbox's layoffs, ironically hurting the cash shop
summary: The MMO is already reeling from reduced "team bandwidth".
body: Earlier this month, Microsoft gutted The Elder Scrolls Online , with 379 staff across ZeniMax gone . And this is the second time it's happened in two years. As Harvey and I put it a few weeks ago, ESO's developers deserved better . The developer, now claiming to be the same size it was 10 years ago (similar to id Software), has just debuted ESO's new seasonal structure but will have to continue carving that path with reduced capabilities. Mere weeks after these layoffs, and we're already seeing their effects. Community manager Kevin Gbolie posted an update on the ESO forums outlining updates to the Crown Store, the game's premium cash shop. "To help manage workload, we have decided to remove the Web Crown Store from the official elderscrollsonline.com website on Wednesday, July 22," Gbolie explains. "The ability to acquire crowns via the website will remain and, of course, this does not affect the items that will be offered on the Crown Store in The Elder Scrolls Online, you will just need to login into the game to view and purchase the latest offerings." title: Nvidia Rubin will 'soak up NAND supply like a sponge absorbs water' says one analyst, thanks to the AI server's new way of using SSDs
summary: It looks like context memory storage (CXM) will guzzle a lot of NAND.
body: Any time there's potentially positive news about storage and memory supply—such as increasing supply from China —I can't help but think 'sure, but the AI industry will guzzle through it regardless.' Not only is demand seemingly insatiable, but sometimes new developments that encourage even more memory use come along. Case in point here with Nvidia Rubin. As SE Daily reports (machine translated), Nvidia's new 'context memory storage' (CMX) tech , which should be here with its new Rubin servers later this year, is going to chomp through a whole bunch of NAND. "Nvidia's CMX is equipped with 576 SSDs, providing a total storage capacity of 9,600 TB. The industry anticipates that the demand for NAND required for CMX will surge from 35 million TB this year to over 100 million TB next year." Analyst Jukan puts these figures into context on X : "Nvidia’s Rubin CMX will quite literally soak up NAND supply like a sponge absorbs water. To put 100 million TB into perspective, it is roughly equivalent to adding another Apple-sized source of demand to the NAND market." Samsung is apparently attempting to establish itself as a key supplier to Nvidia for this new kind of demand. “Nvidia’s CMX is e…

summary: The end of the story or just the start of a fresh chapter?
body: One reason I'm so anti-generative AI is because many models rely on a data set containing written or visual work used without the original creator's permission. As a writer myself, it's a small comfort then that the copyright suit brought against Anthropic has been settled in favour of the author plaintiffs. Anthropic leveraged a library of millions of pirated books to train its Claude chatbot and, as a result, the AI company is now set to pay out a copyright settlement of $1.5 billion (via Euro News ). Some of the affected authors argued this pay out was too small but, as of July 20, a US judge has now approved the deal, closing the book on one of the largest copyright class action lawsuits in US history. Anthropic agreed to pay the $1.5 billion settlement back in September 2025 , but a US judge only approved the deal this week. The settlement covers an approximate total of 500,000 works, with affected authors and publishers to be paid $3,000 for each pirated work. Anthropic says that 91% of those eligible have now filed a claim. Flicking back to the beginning of this saga, writers Andrea Bartz, Charles Graeber, and Kirk Wallace Johnson originally brought their lawsuit against An…

title: Valve puts some elbow grease into Steam Remote Play—maybe one day you won't have to install additional tools to get the best in-home game streaming experience
summary: I want 59.94 frames precisely, waiter.
body: I don't think I've encountered anyone, in the wild, who actually uses Steam's Remote Play game streaming feature since the days of the hardware Steam Link (and what tremendous days they were). In 2026, I think most people making use of in-home streaming tend to default to programs like Apollo and Moonlight. But Valve keeps working on it, perhaps with renewed vigour owing to the recent release of the Steam Machine and upcoming release of the Steam Frame . In a new update for the Steam client, Valve introduced some new fixes and features for Remote Play, while also bringing in patches for Steam Input, the Steam overlay, the Workshop, and various other gubbins. Steam Remote Play will now let you set a framerate limit of 59.94. This remarkably specific framerate is common in, well, televisions—the kind you might want to stream your videogames to from a desktop PC. Previously, if you had configured Steam to output with a framerate limit of 60 fps, there'd be an ever-so-slight discrepancy between that output and what your 59.94 fps TV was capable of displaying, leading to dropped frames. Speaking of which, Remote Play has also been tweaked in such a way that it has "Reduced frame drops …

summary: A situation nobody wants.
body: The Steam Deck was once a budget champion, but no longer. Ever since the price hikes earlier this year, Valve's handheld has become difficult to argue for as a value proposition—and according to recent sales estimates by Boiling Steam , it appears that consumers may agree. The site has attempted to estimate the number of Steam Deck units sold by tracking its position on the Steam Top Sellers listing, and extrapolating data in relation to the entries above and below it (via XDA ). This back-of-the-napkin maths is hardly definitive, but it suggests that when the Steam Deck dropped from around fourth and fifth on the chart position to between tenth and fifteenth after the price adjustments in May of this year, the drop in weekly units sold would equate to roughly 80-82%. Boiling Steam suggests that a slip to fourteenth on the Top Sellers list from its previous top five position represents a drop in gross revenue of approximately 72%, something the outlet characterises as a "complete collapse in total sales." "Starting today, we'll be testing adjustments to our matchmaking algorithm aimed at improving the quality of your matches," a dev explains in an official blog post . Shortly after the Vault Breaker update went live, SteamDB charts showed that Marathon's concurrent player count had jumped to over 14,800 players. That's not a massive number compared to its peers on Steam's Most Played ranking , but it's roughly triple the activity that Marathon pulled at similar times of day throughout the last week—and the most concurrent player activity since player numbers started bleeding after the June free weekend. Vault Breaker trades Marathon's typical extraction loop for an alternative progression scheme. Unlike in normal excursions, runners in Vault Breaker won't keep any gear they pick up and extract with during a run. Instead, the goal is to loot Vault Data that can be used for persistent upgrades to your initial Vault Breaker kit or spent on gear that can be carried into standard runs, offering a more roguelite framework for Bungie's gunplay. …

body: Two years after boasting about how hard it was going on videogames, Hasbro has recorded a "$56 million non-cash impairment" in its second-quarter financial report , arising from the cancellation of several games in development that were expected to release "in 2028 and beyond." First things first: As PC Gamer's Joshua Wolens explained when Sony took a staggering $766 million impairment against Bungie, that $56 million figure isn't a direct loss. Rather, it's an unexpected decrease in value of an asset compared to its book value, separate from standard depreciation. Just a few years ago, inspired by the massive success of Baldur's Gate 3, Hasbro was going hard on videogames. In 2022 it had a half-dozen game studios working on different projects in its stable; two years later Dan Ayoub, head of digital product development at Hasbro-owned Wizards of the Coast, put a number on that effort, saying "we've got over $1 billion in games right now being developed," and adding that "videogames are an integral part of Hasbro's strategy going into the next 100 years." title: For the first time, Unity demonstrates 'an Unreal character running through a Unity world'

body: Unity has demonstrated a Unity Engine game running inside of an Unreal Engine game, marking a step in the direction of Epic Games CEO Tim Sweeney's vision for an interconnected gaming ecosystem. The demonstration took place this week at the Unite Seoul keynote, where Unity also announced the next version of its game engine. Unity's goal with the project, in partnership with Epic, is to allow Unity developers to publish their games in Fortnite. "You will see, for the first time, a Unity game rendering natively inside Unreal Engine," said Unity senior director of technical product management James Stone as he launched the demo. "Now this is not live in Fortnite yet, but it shows what's possible for a shared foundation. "What you're seeing here is a game simulated in Unity, rendered with Unreal. Now, that's not video streamed, there's no smoke and mirrors. This is all real time. But it's not just rendering. Watch the avatar. That's an Unreal character running through a Unity world. That's physics, lighting, input: all of it synchronized between engines." A modder named 0xBobby is hard at work to rid Starfield's takeoff sequences of the loading screen scourge. Seamless Planet Takeoffs is a mod for Starfield that makes departing its planets an at least somewhat immersive experience. body: A hacker going by the name of ByteToBreach has broken in to Romania's cadastre, or land registry, and reportedly wiped information from its databases. Sources speaking to Risky Business have told the outlet that the hacker entered the system using valid credentials, exfiltrated the data within, and later wiped info from its servers after a failed extortion attempt. ByteToBreach is reported to be the same hacker that breached the Swedish government's e-portal earlier this year, among other alleged acts of hackery. The stolen data was said to be announced for sale on two forums. In the meantime, the country's real-estate market appears to have been heavily disrupted . Romanian news outlet Jurnalul De Arges reports a post originating from notary Ana Stan that reads: "I am a notary. Since Tuesday I cannot issue a land registry extract, I cannot authenticate a sale, I cannot register a mortgage." While citizens and notaries are unable to obtain proof of ownership, new real-estate transactions have been brought to a shuddering halt. title: It's probably time to update 7-Zip as the app has just been patched to fix a pretty big vulnerability
summary: It will need to be manually updated to version 26.02 or later.
body: If you use 7-Zip for your file compression and decompression you might want to update, as there's been a pretty big vulnerability patched. And yes, I mean manually update, as 7-Zip doesn't auto-update. You can download and run a new installer from the 7-Zip website . If you're updated to version 26.02 (or later, if you're reading this in the future) then you'll be fine. To check this, go to Help > About 7-Zip in the app. The vulnerability, as explained by Zero Day Initiative (via TechSpot ), "allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code on affected installations of 7-Zip. User interaction is required to exploit this vulnerability in that the target must visit a malicious page or open a malicious file. "The specific flaw exists within the processing of XZ chunked data. Crafted XZ-compressed data can trigger an overflow of a heap-based buffer. An attacker can leverage this vulnerability to execute code in the context of the current process." It sounds like you'd have to try to use a compromised compressed file in 7-Zip for the exploit to work. This might initially sound like a bit of a nothing burger because compromised files can obviously compromise your PC (duh). But I'd say …

title: TSMC admits it's 'jealous' of memory chipmakers' margins, but is limiting its own price hike because its customers would not survive price increases of 'four to five times'
summary: The company announces a 10 - 15% price increase, but isn't keen to emulate the memory industry's runaway pricing.
body: You can be sure that whatever TSMC does next, the rest of the industry will follow. Unfortunately, it looks like this major player in chip manufacturing is set to raise its prices by as much as 10% in 2027. This is due to both the rising cost of materials as well as the company's overseas fab construction projects (such as the already booked up though not even built yet 2nm fab in Arizona ). Price increases ranging between 5 to 10% will affect various chip production services offered by TSMC (via Nikkei Asia ). Sources also claim that extra orders of high-performance computing chips not otherwise accounted for in customers' original forecasts will incur a 10 to 15% premium on top. The services affected include TSMC's 7nm and more advanced fabrication processes. As TSMC accounts for over 70% of the global semiconductor foundry market , Big Tech will have little choice but to pay the bill when it's due. Waiting until the start of 2027 to raise its prices is considered a less aggressive move within the industry (especially in light of memory prices being predicted to rise by as much as 50% in Q3 of 2026). Indeed, we've already seen more than our fair share of price increases this yea…

title: Arctic Freezer 36-S review
summary: Surprisingly effective for the money.
body: The Arctic Freezer 36-S lands at the right time. Prices are rising for PC builds but you can claw some money back with this cooler. It costs just $22 / £19 . A small price to pay for ample cooling for your processor. And it is, genuinely, good. Arctic has plenty of accolades to its name when it comes to cooling. One of its more popular designs, the Arctic Freezer 36 , is our pick for the best budget air cooler . With two fans, an effective heatsink design, and a great price tag, it's all anyone needs to chill most of today's popular gaming chips. But could you make do with less? That's what the Freezer 36-S puts to the test. A few cuts have been made to the Freezer 36-S compared to the Freezer 36, including trimming down to just one fan. But it still has a lot going for it, especially when it comes to performance, as you can see in the charts below . The Freezer 36-S performs really well considering its size and price tag. Though price is a sticking point: the Freezer 36-S is going up against the best and when you can get the Freezer 36 for very nearly the same cost, it's tough to argue for the slightly cheaper model. title: Acer's Predator Atlas 8 pricing puts the nail in the handheld coffin for me: it is now a luxury market
summary: Just as predicted.
body: A couple of months ago, Intel announced a couple of new chips for handheld gaming PCs, the Intel Arc G3 and G3 Extreme. It was certainly exciting to have something new on that front, given we've been seeing mostly the same silicon crammed inside such devices for a very long time. But at the time I warned that signs pointed towards yet more prohibitively expensive price tags . And now we're seeing Acer's pricing for its new Predator Atlas 8 handhelds, it's safe to say I was unfortunately right. As spotted by KitGuru and confirmed by yours truly , the UK pricing for these handhelds, containing new Intel Panther Lake chips and expected to launch in October this year, ranges from £1,000 to £1,500. The cheapest has a regular G3 chip, 16 GB of memory, and a 512 GB SSD. You can up the memory to 24 GB for another £100. A G3 Extreme version with a 1 TB SSD and 24 GB of memory will be able to be nabbed for £1,300, and bumping that up to 32 GB of RAM will get you up to the top £1,500 price tag. Converting those figures to dollars, that makes for a handheld that ranges from (roughly) $1,300 to $2,000. Though of course prices don't usually track exchange rates exactly. title: Developer of Burnout spiritual successor Wreckreation has formed a new studio working on a sequel
summary: "We've reached a point where it makes sense for the project to have a new home, one that we own completely."
body: To this day, the now-stagnant Burnout series is still the prime example of an arcade racer that every other contemporary is compared to. Naturally, it's spurred on a bunch of imitators and spiritual successors over the years, especially after the decline of Criterion Games into a support studio at EA. Nowadays, instead of pioneering racing games, it's a "Battlefield Studio" . One such spiritual successor was 2025's Wreckreation, created by Three Fields Entertainment, a small team of veterans, including from Criterion Games, making games since 1999. Unfortunately, Wreckreaction launched to mixed reviews, not quite reaching the heights of its inspiration. But it's not the end of the team or the series. "We've made a double announcement today," the developer's latest statement on Patreon reads. "We're making a sequel to Wreckreation … It's built around aggression, pressure, risk, and of course consequence." "Over the last six months we've been talking directly to players about what they want from a modern arcade driving game, and Wreckreation 2 is the result of those conversations," said studio head Fiona Sperry. title: Valve has replaced a Steam Machine for free after it was stolen by porch pirates, saving one UK redditor roughly £1,000
summary: A feel-good story? What year is this again?
body: The Steam Machine is a pricey piece of hardware. Which is why UK redditor mdumtshali must have been heartbroken (or very annoyed) when theirs was stolen from their doorstep after the Royal Mail dropped it off while they were at work. The delivery driver sent a picture of the Steam Machine box to prove it had been delivered, and even tried to cover it with a plastic bag (via Notebookcheck ). But alas, when mdumtshali returned home, porch pirates (my new favourite term) had already absconded with the ill-fated mini PC. However, mdumtshali says they eventually contacted Steam Support, which duly agreed to send out a replacement Steam Machine free of charge. "Honestly, kudos to Steam Support," says the redditor. "[I] thought I was going to be out £1000, which I really can't afford." Steam Support initially asked mdumtshali to check with neighbours to see if the parcel had been taken in, which they duly did to no avail. As the Steam Machine is a high-value item, it's surprising that a signature wasn't required by the Royal Mail for delivery confirmation, which would have likely prevented this whole fiasco in the first place. title: Ex-TSMC manager first to be indicted under National Security Act for allegedly attempting to leak core Taiwanese chipmaking technologies to China
summary: They could face up to seven years in prison.
body: Taiwanese prosecutors have reportedly indicted a former TSMC manager for allegedly stealing trade secrets to give to China. This case, according to a spokesperson from Taiwan's High Prosecutors Office who relayed the information to Nikkei Asia , is the first of its kind: "Although the attempt to transfer the technology to China was unsuccessful, the case remains highly significant as the first prosecution under the National Security Act involving an alleged attempt to leak Taiwan's national core technologies to China." The plan was, says Nikkei Asia, "to establish a semiconductor materials analysis company in China called CSMAC." Apparently, the prosecutors discovered that Chen, a former TSMC deputy manager, worked with another Hong Kong man to steal TSMC trade secrets, copying 21 confidential documents, taking them home to study for use in China. The prosecutors also claim that Chen was considering recruiting more talent from Taiwan for CSMAC. Chen has been detained for the past couple of months, and could face up to seven years in prison. TSMC, on the other hand, is apparently doing fine, as it has recovered all the documents. This time last year I reported that the Taiwanese ch…

title: Activision should ditch the Call of Duty movie and bring back its absurd celeb fest where Will Arnett went on a quest to find his own junk
summary: Ridley Scott, Will Arnett, Jason Bateman, and Andrew Santino all made Call of Duty shows before.
body: With the news that Call of Duty , of all things, is getting adapted into a movie by the director of Battleship —and it's unfortunately set in the Modern Warfare universe —I got vivid flashbacks to the series' last attempt in multimedia. Back in 2011, Activision decided it was time to change how it would support Call of Duty games post-launch. Modern Warfare 3 (the first one) saw the introduction of Call of Duty Elite, a subscription service that included monthly content like new maps, as well as social network tools. While there was a free, lite version which included things like Theater Mode and career stats, the real deal cost $50 a year. Of course, the monthly DLC (later sold as separate packs for non-subscribers) was the reason most people, myself included, ever joined up. But Activision was really pushing for it to be a social network-inspired multimedia platform, so it sweetened the deal with three video series, one by Ridley and Tony Scott, another by Will Arnett and Jason Bateman, and one from Andrew Santino. title: Redditor uses 'ticking time bomb' vial of gallium to stop their GPU sagging
summary: 'It was the only thing I found that had almost the perfect height.'
body: Behold, Anti-sag temperature indicator and ticking time bomb from r/pcmasterrace For a nervy PC Gamer such as myself, cracking open your gaming rig for even a spot of light dust busting can feel like you're flirting with danger. Well, one Redditor has decided to laugh in the face of danger by using a tube of gallium to stop their GPU from sagging. Philip_777 shared their unusual anti-sagging intervention, describing it as both a " temperature indicator and ticking time bomb ." For those a little unclear on why this is such a bad idea, allow me to share some of my recent education on the subject. Gallium has a super low melting point, meaning that it will revert to liquid form if you attempt to hold it in the palm of your hand. Apparently, gallium can also 'eat' other metals, like aluminium. In other words, a gallium leak inside of your PC would be a very bad, no good time. Philip_777 has taken some precautions by confining the gallium in what I'm hoping is a sturdy plastic vial. They write in a follow up comment, "It shouldn't become brittle or anything. I think it was HDPE [High-density polyethylene]. Still one of those moments where you know it's safe, but there's still a chance…

title: I'm very into this Papers, Please-esque puzzle game about identifying traitors to the socialist motherland
summary: Hey, it's a living.
body: Citizens! We live in times of unprecedented danger! We are beset on all sides by traitors and saboteurs. Reactionaries whisper treason in dimly lit rooms. Their mouths spew poison and lies that eat away at the foundations of the worker-peasant revolution, at the root of the world revolution itself. And, worst of all, no one gave me a desk big enough to deal with it. (Image credit: JKBC) What Awaits, Aleksey? is a clever mystery/puzzle game that caters to all my specific obsessions. It's a Stasi simulator, more or less—you are Aleksey Lutomski, a recent hire at the security service of a fictional 20th-century communist state, tasked with identifying the members of an underground cell of dissidents who are going around saying mean things about the motherland. For the most part, this means obsessing over the tidiness of your desk. It is no longer World War 2 and you are not Stierlitz . You are not kicking in doors and taking down fascist spies with a Tokarev. It is 1970: the socialist sixth of the Earth has settled into bleary stagnation. title: Glorious GHS InfinitePlay review
summary: This versatile set of cans impresses with its hot swappable battery system, but it's just okay otherwise.
body: Glorious, purveyor of PC peripherals such as gaming keyboards and mice, has branched out into headsets. The company released its first wired effort, the Glorious GHS Eternal , earlier this year and, in terms of build quality, the wireless InfinitePlay shares a lot of DNA. In terms of pricing though, the two headsets couldn't be more different. The swappable battery system goes some way to justify the significant jump in price. As someone who loves wireless headsets, but doesn't love how often they have to become tethered to my PC to charge, this proved a real game-changer. The InfinitePlay is also designed to be a versatile bit of kit you can take everywhere. Whether you're listening to tunes on the go, or counting the shots in one of the best FPS games , the InfinitePlay's balanced sound profile is tuned to handle both scenarios equally well. EQ tweaking is offered via the straightforward to use Glorious Core software, with more in-depth options planned for a future update. That versatility is backed up by great connectivity. You've got the usual choice between a Bluetooth, or 2.4 GHz connection, and the dongle for the latter comes with a USB-C to USB-A adapter. As such, it's jus…

title: Nvidia finally reveals what controls devs actually have for DLSS 5, and don't worry folks, there are TWO main sliders to control the AI filter
summary: The company is, however, 'building more' controls.
body: Nvidia announced DLSS 5 back in March and it received, let's say, mixed reactions . A lot of people weren't happy about how, judging by the demos , it seemed to essentially just slap a 'yassifying' AI filter on top of a game scene. Nvidia itself confirmed that all the tech has to work with for each AI frame is a traditional 2D frame and motion vectors, essentially confirming the "filter" idea. Now, at SIGGRAPH 2026, the company has explained that developers will get two main sliders to control DLSS 5's intensity. My initial reaction was probably the same as yours: two whole sliders, whoop-de-doo. It's hardly a massive improvement from the one on-to-off masking slider we previously thought devs would have access to. But let's take a look at what they're actually controlling and how it works before we jump to conclusions. The two sliders are for 'structural intensity' and 'tone intensity'. Despite what the word "structure" might seem to imply, Nvidia is keen to point out that "it is not changing the geometry in any way, and it is uplifting the image." The structural intensity slider in fact is "modulating the high-frequency detail of the frame." This includes things such as ambient …

title: Developer creates a password-cracking Game Boy Advance that is 'about thirty million times slower than a modern cracking rig'
summary: Why? Why not?
body: A security engineer has "ported" one of the best known password recovery utilities to the GameBoy Advance. The port is based on Hashcat, a password recovery utility that otherwise pitches itself as the "world's fastest." But I'm doing the bunny ears around 'ported' because even the developer admits this is "not really" Hashcat in full. It turns out the 21-year-old Nintendo handheld console is also not really the best hardware for cracking passwords. "The monstrously powerful 16.78 MHz ARM7TDMI chip does an astronomical 727 SHA256 hashes per second!! Which is about thirty million times slower than a modern cracking rig," Project developer Mark El-Khoury jokes on LinkedIn , "350 days of continuous cracking on this bad boy could be done in about 1 second with a modern GPU-accelerated cracking rig." Even with its snail's pace, I can't help but dream up a heist movie where the would-be thieves only use repurposed gaming hardware like this to crack open password-protected vaults. I can just picture the scene now: 'How much longer is this gonna take?' 'Uh, well, you may want to pitch up a tent…?' Riveting. title: A new Elder Scrolls is still years away, but this fun Arthurian take on the formula is 50% off right now
summary: Tainted Grail: The Fall of Avalon is a very competent Bethesda RPG, not made by Bethesda.
body: It's a little surprising there aren't more The Elder Scrolls clones. Aside from Avowed, the only other example I can think of is Tainted Grail: The Fall of Avalon. It launched in May last year after a stint in early access, and the response was positive, perhaps surprisingly so, considering how tough it must be for a relatively young studio to make a game of this scale. Our reviewer wrote that it "puts an interesting spin on Arthurian legend and presents a competent world that plays like a love letter to the open-world first-person RPG genre." It's a "dark reimagination" of Arthurian legends, set entirely on the island of Avalon. There are three distinct zones to explore—in this sense, it wends closer to Avowed—and it has all the usual distractions: you can fish, farm, cook, mine, smith weapons, mix spells, and sketch in your journal, between the usual bouts of dungeon crawling, overworld exploration, and "morally ambiguous" choice making. I've played a couple of hours on Steam Deck and it runs pretty well there too, albeit at around the 30-45fps mark. The art style is darker than Skyrim's, coming off almost as a hybrid of Dark Souls and Morrowind. It feels unmistakably like a fir…
